splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate an employer's withdrawal liability from a multiemployer pension plan to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year, or whether they may be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing for more current data to be used in calculations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the court-ordered supervision period beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year and raises questions about the limits of judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the period during which violations can be found and punished.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the supervision term runs and expires as specified in the judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1)—which categorically bars raising any previously presented claim—when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners face a stricter bar than Congress may have intended, in addition to § 2255(h)'s separate requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the § 2244(b)(1) bar does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity takes priority over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations when a criminal sentencing provision is ambiguous. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while at least three other circuits hold that lenity prevails. The issue has gained new significance after the Supreme Court's decision in Loper Bright overruling Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability without clear congressional authorization.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations takes priority over the rule of lenity, allowing the Commission's anti-defendant reading of ambiguous Guidelines provisions to control.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any single criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision means a defendant is disqualified from relief only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria, or whether meeting any one criterion is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentencing outcomes depending on the circuit in which a defendant is prosecuted.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any single criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' distributively or disjunctively.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt), such that Application Note 1 extending the definition to cover inchoate offenses is not entitled to deference.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes conspiracy and other inchoate offenses within the definition of 'controlled substance offense' for career offender purposes, is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor. Some circuits defer to the note, while others hold the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses. The Sentencing Commission has since amended the guideline to move the note into the text, but the split remains relevant for cases sentenced under earlier versions.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) is ambiguous as to inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy and attempt is a reasonable interpretation entitled to controlling deference.

positions.label: Application Note 1 exceeds the guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline's scope and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. The Eighth Circuit in Crandall held that non-retroactive changes cannot contribute at all, while the Seventh Circuit in Thacker held similarly. Other circuits have permitted consideration of such changes, and the Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to authorize it, creating further tension.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), whether alone or in combination with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state-controlled substance is not listed on the federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) encompasses state drug convictions for substances not controlled under federal law. Some circuits hold the definition broadly encompasses any state drug offense, while others require the substance to be federally scheduled. This affects career offender designations and base offense levels for firearms offenses.

positions.label: State convictions qualify regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines broadly includes state drug offenses even if the underlying substance is not on the federal controlled substances schedules, because the text refers to 'an offense under state law.'

positions.label: State convictions must involve federally scheduled substances
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if it involves a substance that is also controlled under federal law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation occurring after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 becomes part of the bankruptcy estate. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other courts have held it belongs to the debtor. This affects the fundamental bargain of Chapter 13 conversions and whether debtors can protect increased home equity.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a), post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects good-faith converting debtors by limiting the converted estate to property as of the original filing date, meaning post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re Castleman (as described in dissent)

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order on a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment, given that such a motion is filed in a criminal case but seeks a civil remedy.
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether an appeal from a Hyde Amendment fee order is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the shorter criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, but acknowledged the split with circuits treating it as criminal.

positions.label: Civil appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because a Hyde Amendment motion is filed in the criminal case, the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly consider punishment as a purpose of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release, or whether revocation sentences may only serve the purpose of sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts revoking supervised release may impose sentences to punish the defendant for the underlying violation conduct, or only to sanction the breach of the court's trust. The Sixth Circuit allows express punishment at revocation, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split where other circuits limit the permissible purposes of revocation sentences.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violation conduct when revoking supervised release, not just for the breach of trust.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust, not punishing the underlying violation conduct, consistent with the distinct purposes of supervised release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker's selection of a motor carrier results in injury. This affects liability allocation across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation, falling within the FAAAA's broad preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent selection of carriers are not preempted because they do not directly regulate the broker's prices, routes, or services.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Aspen American Insurance Co. v. Landstar Ranger, Inc.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to state voting procedures. The question determines whether such disputes must wait until after an election, when they may be moot or create chaos, or whether candidates may challenge election rules prospectively.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that allegedly dilute votes or alter election outcomes, allowing pre-election challenges.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's speculative and generalized allegations about ballot-counting procedures do not establish a concrete injury sufficient for Article III standing.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may be used as a vehicle to address potential constitutional or legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence — issues that typically must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255. Some circuits allow these factors to be considered, while others hold that § 3582 cannot serve as a substitute for § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider the legal or constitutional infirmity of the underlying conviction or sentence as a factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a proper vehicle to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments?
splits.description: The circuits have taken different approaches to the standards for establishing liability when business operators discriminate against or unfairly treat customers. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach, indicating a circuit split on the relevant legal questions affecting hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: The Second Circuit applied its own standards for evaluating discrimination claims in hospitality/business establishment contexts, which the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for evaluating discrimination or unfair treatment claims against business establishments, creating a split that prompted SCOTUS review.
